tables.raw_text:
|  |  | shall be considered according to the delta equivalent amount of the underlying or of the notional
underlying.
Actual modified duration for each contract may be computed using the contracted rate as coupon
and the relevant yield curve for discounting factor.
Alternatively all interest rate derivatives can also be dealt with in the following manner:
 | i) | FRAs | Forward Rate Agreements (FRAs) could also be considered as a combination of a short position
and a long position. For instance, a long position in a September three month FRA (taken on June
1), can be bucketed as a short position in a bond with a maturity of 6 months and a long position in
a bond with a maturity of 3 months. Accordingly a liability in the 3-6 months bucket and an asset in
the 28 days to 3 months bucket may be shown. The amount to be reckoned for computing interest
rate sensitivity is the notional value of the FRA.
 | ii) | Swaps | Interest Rate Swaps could be considered as a combination of a short position and a long position.
The notional of the fixed and floating leg of an Interest Rate Swap could be shown in the respective
maturity bucket based on the maturity date for the fixed leg and the reset date for the floating leg.
Suppose a bank receives 5-year fixed and pays floating MIBOR, then the fixed leg of the swap could
be shown as an asset in the '5-7 year' bucket and the floating leg would be shown as a liability in
'1-28days' bucket. Similarly, a currency swap may be considered as a combination of a short
position in one currency and long position in another currency. The two positions will be sensitive
to the changes in the respective interest rates. The notionals of the two currencies will be bucketed
as a short / long positions in the respective currency with relevant maturity.
 | iii) | Futures | Interest Rate Futures (IRFs) could also be considered as a combination of a short position and long
position. For instance, a long position in a September three month IRF (taken on June 1), can be
bucketed as a long position in Government bond, with a maturity of six months and a short position
in Government bond with maturity of three months. The amount to be reckoned for computing
interest rate sensitivity is the notional value of the IRF.
tables.markdown:
|  |  |  | shall be considered according to the delta equivalent amount of the underlying or of the notional
underlying.
Actual modified duration for each contract may be computed using the contracted rate as coupon
and the relevant yield curve for discounting factor.
Alternatively all interest rate derivatives can also be dealt with in the following manner: |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | i) | FRAs | Forward Rate Agreements (FRAs) could also be considered as a combination of a short position
and a long position. For instance, a long position in a September three month FRA (taken on June
1), can be bucketed as a short position in a bond with a maturity of 6 months and a long position in
a bond with a maturity of 3 months. Accordingly a liability in the 3-6 months bucket and an asset in
the 28 days to 3 months bucket may be shown. The amount to be reckoned for computing interest
rate sensitivity is the notional value of the FRA. |
|  | ii) | Swaps | Interest Rate Swaps could be considered as a combination of a short position and a long position.
The notional of the fixed and floating leg of an Interest Rate Swap could be shown in the respective
maturity bucket based on the maturity date for the fixed leg and the reset date for the floating leg.
Suppose a bank receives 5-year fixed and pays floating MIBOR, then the fixed leg of the swap could
be shown as an asset in the '5-7 year' bucket and the floating leg would be shown as a liability in
'1-28days' bucket. Similarly, a currency swap may be considered as a combination of a short
position in one currency and long position in another currency. The two positions will be sensitive
to the changes in the respective interest rates. The notionals of the two currencies will be bucketed
as a short / long positions in the respective currency with relevant maturity. |
|  | iii) | Futures | Interest Rate Futures (IRFs) could also be considered as a combination of a short position and long
position. For instance, a long position in a September three month IRF (taken on June 1), can be
bucketed as a long position in Government bond, with a maturity of six months and a short position
in Government bond with maturity of three months. The amount to be reckoned for computing
interest rate sensitivity is the notional value of the IRF. |

children.title: Annex - VII: Guidance on Bucketing (p.168)
children.text:
Annex - VII: Guidance on Bucketing
Guidelines on Bucketing of various items of Assets and Liabilities in the Interest Rate Sensitivity Statement, along with the
coupons and yields to be used
Sr.
Liabilities
Framework for Bucketing of Assets / Liabilities / Off Balance Sheet Items and
Computation of Modified Duration
1.
Capital - Equity shares
Non-sensitive for TGA.
Not to be bucketed for DGA.
2.
Reserves and Surplus
Non-sensitive for TGA.

Not to be bucketed for DGA.
3.
(i)
Innovative
Perpetual Debt
Instruments (IPDI) eligible for
Tier I status

Sensitive.
(ii)
Debt
capital
Instruments
qualifying as Upper Tier II
Capital and Tier II bonds
Bucketing as per residual maturity / re-pricing.
(iii)
Preference shares eligible for
Tier I and Tier II Capital
Coupon rate: Contract rate.
Yield: Govt. of India yield for corresponding period with appropriate mark up for rated
bonds (corresponding to rating of the instrument) published by FBIL.
4.
(i)
Current Deposits
Sensitive.

A bank better equipped to estimate the behavioral pattern of current deposits should
classify them in the appropriate buckets based on behavioral maturity as per the
behavioural study. In such cases to compute the Modified Duration, a bank must use
its relevant term deposit rates as the discount rate, coupon rate being zero.
However, a bank which have not conducted the above behavioral study may classify
15 per cent of the current deposits as volatile and place it in the first time bucket (viz.,
1-28 days) and 85 per cent may be placed in the 1-3 years time bucket.
Coupon Rate: Zero.
Yield:
(i)
As the mid-point of the 1-28 days time bucket is 14 days, a bank could take
its 14 days term deposit rate as the yield to compute the MD of the volatile
portion.
children.summary: Sets out bucketing treatment for capital and deposit liabilities for MD computation: equity capital and reserves are non-sensitive for TGA and excluded from DGA bucketing; IPDI (Tier I) is sensitive; Upper Tier II/Tier II instruments are bucketed by residual maturity/re-pricing; preference shares use contract coupon and GOI yield plus FBIL-published rating-based mark-up. For current deposits, banks may bucket based on behavioural studies; absent such studies, 15% is treated as volatile in 1–28 days and 85% as core in 1–3 years, with zero coupon and yields based on the bank’s 14-day and 2-year term deposit rates (mid-points).
children.description: Bucketing and coupon/yield conventions for capital instruments and current deposits (including default behavioural splits).
topics: preference shares goi yield fbil markup
modified duration discount rate term deposit rates

children.title: Annex - VII: Guidance on Bucketing (p.169)
children.text:
Sr.
Liabilities
Framework for Bucketing of Assets / Liabilities / Off Balance Sheet Items and
Computation of Modified Duration
(ii)
As the mid-point of the 1-3 years time bucket is 2 years, a bank could take
its 2-year term deposit rate as the discount rate to compute the Modified
Duration of the core portion.
(ii)
Savings Bank Deposits

Sensitive.
A bank may estimate the future behaviour/ sensitivity of savings bank deposits to
changes in market variables, the sensitivity so estimated could be shown under
appropriate time buckets. The existing savings bank rate may be used as coupon and
the bank’s own relevant term deposit rates must be used as the yield to compute the
MD.
However, where a bank has not undertaken any behavioral study it may include core
portion (say 90 per cent) as rate sensitive and include the same in 1-3 years time
bucket. The volatile portion (10 per cent) may be placed in 1-28 days bucket.
Coupon Rate: Existing Savings Bank interest rate, i.e., 3.5 per cent.
Yield:
(i)
As the mid-point of the 1-28 days time bucket is 14 days, a bank could take
its 14 days term deposit rate as the yield to compute the MD of the volatile
portion.
(ii)
As the mid-point of the 1-3 years time bucket is 2 years, a bank could take
its 2-year term deposit rate as the discount rate to compute the Modified
Duration of the core portion.
(iii)
Term deposits
Sensitive.
A bank may study the behavioural pattern of large value fixed rate term deposits to
arrive at the percentage of deposits encashed/ foreclosed and renewed before
maturity, i.e the quantum on which the option is exercised. The amount of deposits
which are estimated to be prone to pre-mature withdrawal as per such studies may
be placed in the corresponding maturity buckets.
The other fixed rate term deposits may be distributed in various time buckets on the
basis of remaining term to maturity.

In the case of floating rate term deposits, the amounts may be shown under the time
bucket when the deposits contractually become due for re-pricing.
Coupon rate: A bank may compute the average coupon on the term deposits by
comparing the interest paid / accrued during the relevant accounting period on term
deposits to the monthly average outstanding term deposits.
children.summary: Prescribes bucketing and MD inputs for savings bank deposits and term deposits. Savings deposits are sensitive; banks may allocate across buckets based on behavioural sensitivity studies using savings rate as coupon and own term deposit rates as yield; absent studies, 90% core is placed in 1–3 years and 10% volatile in 1–28 days, with coupon at the existing savings rate (3.5%) and yields using 14-day and 2-year term deposit rates (bucket mid-points). Term deposits are sensitive; fixed-rate term deposits may be adjusted for premature withdrawal/renewal behaviour (option exercise) based on studies, otherwise bucketed by remaining maturity; floating-rate term deposits are bucketed to the contractual re-pricing date, with coupon computed as an average based on interest accrued/paid over monthly average outstanding.
children.description: Behavioural bucketing rules and coupon/yield methodology for savings deposits and fixed/floating term deposits.
topics: modified duration yield term deposit rates

children.title: Annex - VII: Guidance on Bucketing (p.170)
children.text:
Sr.
Liabilities
Framework for Bucketing of Assets / Liabilities / Off Balance Sheet Items and
Computation of Modified Duration

Yield: A bank's card interest rate for deposits for the relevant term may be used.
(iv)
Certificates of Deposit
Sensitive and re-prices on maturity.
The amounts should be distributed to different buckets on the basis of remaining term
to maturity. However, in case of floating rate CDs, the amounts may be shown under
the time bucket when CDs contractually become due for re-pricing.

Coupon rate: Calculated in a similar manner as term deposits.
Yield: Govt. of India yield for corresponding period with mark up for rated bonds
(corresponding to CD ratings of the bank) published by FIMMDA may be taken as
yield.
5.
Borrowings
(i)
Money at Call and Short
Notice

The amounts should be distributed to different buckets on the basis of remaining
maturity / re-pricing date.

Overnight call money rate may be taken as both the coupon and yield.
(ii)
Inter-bank (Term)

The amounts should be distributed to different buckets on the basis of remaining
maturity / re-pricing date.

The coupon will be as per actual rate for each inter-bank term loan and yield may be
based on the FBIL-NSE MIBOR curve, with appropriate mark up as per rating of the
Tier II bonds of the bank.
(iii)


Refinances

The amounts should be distributed to different buckets on the basis of remaining
maturity in the case of fixed rate refinances and re-pricing date for floating rate
refinances.

The appropriate refinance rate of RBI, NHB, NABARD, etc. may be used as the
coupon and yield may be based on the GOI securities of corresponding tenors.
(iv)
Others (specify)
-
6.
Other Liabilities and Provisions
i)
Bills Payable
Non-sensitive.
ii)
Inter-office adjustment
Non-sensitive.
iii)
Provisions

Non-sensitive.
iv)
Others

Non-sensitive.
7.
Repos (Funds borrowed)
Sensitive.

The amounts should be distributed to different buckets on the basis of remaining
maturity.
children.summary: Continues liability bucketing guidance covering CDs and borrowings and identifies non-sensitive other liabilities. CDs are sensitive and re-price on maturity; bucket by remaining maturity (or contractual re-pricing for floating-rate CDs), with coupon computed like term deposits and yield based on GOI yield plus FIMMDA-published rating-based mark-up. Borrowings (call/short notice, inter-bank term, refinances) are bucketed by remaining maturity/re-pricing; call money uses overnight call rate as coupon and yield; inter-bank term uses actual coupon and yield based on FBIL-NSE MIBOR curve with rating-based mark-up; refinances use the relevant refinance rate (RBI/NHB/NABARD etc.) as coupon and GOI securities of corresponding tenor for yield. Bills payable, inter-office adjustments, provisions and other items under ‘Other Liabilities and Provisions’ are non-sensitive; repos (funds borrowed) are sensitive and bucketed by remaining maturity.
children.description: Bucketing and valuation inputs for CDs, borrowings/refinances, other liabilities, and repos (funds borrowed).
topics: call money overnight rate coupon yield

children.title: Annex - VII: Guidance on Bucketing (p.171)
children.text:
Sr.
Liabilities
Framework for Bucketing of Assets / Liabilities / Off Balance Sheet Items and
Computation of Modified Duration

The coupon will be as per actual rate for each repo and yield may be based on FBILNSE MIBOR curve.
8.
Bills Re-discounted (DUPN)
Sensitive.
The amounts should be distributed to different buckets on the basis of remaining
maturity.

Coupon rate: Appropriate discount rate.
Yield: FBIL-NSE MIBOR curve may be used as the yield, with appropriate mark up as
per rating of the Tier II bonds of the bank.
9.
Forex Swaps (Buy/ Sell)

Sensitive.
Actual MD for each contract may be computed using the ₹ implied rate through
forward premium / discount as both coupon and discount rate.
10.
Others

A.
Total Liabilities


Sr.
Assets
Framework for Bucketing of Assets / Liabilities / Off Balance Sheet Items and Computation
of Modified Duration
1.
Cash
Non-sensitive.
2.
Balances with RBI
Non-sensitive.
3.
Balances with other banks

i)
Current account
Non-sensitive.
ii)
Money at Call and Short
Notice
Sensitive on maturity.
The amount should be plotted in the 1-28 days bucket.
The overnight call money rate may be used as both coupon and yield.
iii)
Term deposits and other
placements
Sensitive.
The amounts should be distributed to different time buckets on the basis of residual maturity or
residual period to repricing, as relevant.
Coupon rate: Relevant rate of term deposit / placement.
Yield: Term deposit rates of the corresponding tenors of the banks with whom deposits are placed.
4.
Investments
(Performing)
(including those under reverse
repos but excluding repos)
Sensitive
For the purpose of bucketing and calculation of Modified Duration, investments may be classified
into SLR and non-SLR investments as indicated below:
children.summary: Specifies MD inputs for repos, bills rediscounted (DUPN), and forex swaps, and begins asset-side bucketing guidance. Repos use actual repo rate as coupon and FBIL-NSE MIBOR curve as yield; bills rediscounted are sensitive, bucketed by remaining maturity with appropriate discount rate as coupon and MIBOR curve plus rating-based mark-up as yield; forex swaps are sensitive with MD computed per contract using the INR implied rate from forward premium/discount as both coupon and discount rate. On assets, cash and balances with RBI are non-sensitive; balances with other banks include non-sensitive current accounts, call/short notice placements sensitive on maturity (plotted in 1–28 days) using overnight call rate as coupon/yield, and term deposits/placements sensitive and bucketed by residual maturity/re-pricing with coupon at the placement rate and yield based on the counterparty banks’ term deposit rates; investments (performing) are treated as sensitive and are to be classified into SLR and non-SLR for bucketing/MD.
children.description: Rules for bucketing and MD computation for repos/DUPN/forex swaps and initial asset categories (cash, RBI, interbank balances, investments).
topics: repos coupon actual rate yield mibor curve
forex swaps implied inr rate forward premium

children.title: Annex - VII: Guidance on Bucketing (p.172)
children.text:
i)
SLR investments
Sensitive.
Actual Modified Duration of each SLR security should be used.
Yield: G-Sec yield curve.
ii)
Non-SLR investments
Sensitive (except equity which may be put in the non-sensitive bucket).
Actual Modified Duration of each Non-SLR security should be used.
Yield: FIMMDA benchmark curve.
iii)
Re-capitalisation bonds
Sensitive.
Actual Modified Duration of each recapitalization bond may be computed.
iv)
Investments in SRs issued
by ARCs
Non-sensitive.
5.
Advances (Performing)
Sensitive.
The amounts should be distributed to different time buckets on the basis of residual maturity or
residual period to re-pricing, as relevant.
Banks may compute the average coupon for the advances portfolio by comparing the interest
income during the relevant accounting period from 'standard' advances to the monthly average
outstanding 'standard' advances.
The average rating of the advances portfolio may be estimated by each bank to arrive at the
applicable yield. One of the methods for estimating the average rating may be as follows:
Multiply the outstanding advances in each bucket with the internal rating scores to arrive at the
weighted average rating of the advances in that bucket. Thereafter, this rating may be mapped to
an external rating. In case a major portion of the bank's advances in a particular time bucket
happens to be unrated, the bank may use the rating scores of large advances / rated advances in
each bucket (mapped with the rating of external agency) for arriving at weighted average rating for
the bucket. On the basis of the average rating of each bucket, the yield may be arrived at using the
FBIL yield curve for GoI securities with appropriate mark-up.
i)
Bills
Purchased
and
Discounted (incl. Bills under
DUPN)
Sensitive on maturity.
The average coupon and yield for the advances portfolio, as computed above, may be used.
ii)
Cash credit / Overdrafts
(incl.
TODs
/
Loans
repayable on demand)
Sensitive on re-pricing / date of next renewal, whichever is earlier. In the case of BPLR / Base Ratelinked advances, a bank may estimate the re-pricing date based on the past experience and future
forecast for the changes in their BPLR / Base Rate.
The average coupon and yield for the advances portfolio, as computed above, may be used.
children.summary: Provides bucketing guidance for interest rate risk sensitivity classification of investments and performing advances. It specifies which items are to be treated as sensitive/non-sensitive, requires use of actual modified duration for SLR/non-SLR/recapitalisation bonds, and prescribes yield curves (G-Sec curve for SLR; FIMMDA benchmark for non-SLR). For performing advances, it prescribes bucketing by residual maturity or residual re-pricing, and outlines methods to compute average coupon and derive bucket-wise yields using internal-to-external rating mapping with FBIL GoI yield curve plus mark-up; bills are sensitive on maturity and CC/OD are sensitive on re-pricing/next renewal (earlier), including estimation for BPLR/Base Rate-linked repricing.
children.description: Rules for bucketing investments and performing advances into sensitive/non-sensitive time buckets, including duration and yield curve selection.

children.title: Annex - VII: Guidance on Bucketing (p.173)
children.text:
iii)
Term Loans
Sensitive on re-pricing / maturity, whichever is earlier. In the case of BPLR / Base Rate linked
advances, a bank may estimate the re-pricing date based on the past experience and future forecast
for the changes in their BPLR / Base Rate.
The average coupon and yield for the advances portfolio, as computed above, may be used.
6.
NPAs
(Advances
and
Investments) *
Sensitive.
Sub-standard NPAs should be slotted in the 1-3 years time bucket.
Doubtful and Loss Assets - should be slotted in the 3-5 years time bucket.
Coupon: The coupon rate will be taken as zero.
The yield curve prescribed by FIMMDA for unrated exposures/ default category may be used as
yield.
7.
Fixed Assets
Non-sensitive.
8.
i)
Inter-office adjustment
Non-sensitive.
ii)
Leased Assets
Sensitive on cash flows.
The amounts should be distributed to respective maturity buckets corresponding to the cash flow
dates.
Yield curve prescribed by FIMMDA for valuation of corporate bonds as per the average rating
estimated for leased assets to be used for arriving at the yields.
The average coupon for the leased assets portfolio, as computed for advances, may be used.
iii)
Others
Non-sensitive.
9.
Reverse Repos (Funds Lent)
Sensitive. The amounts should be distributed to different buckets on the basis of remaining maturity.
The coupon will be as per actual rate for each repo and yield may be based on FBIL-NSE MIBOR
curve.
10.
Forex Swaps (Sell / Buy)
Sensitive.
Actual MD for each contract may be computed using the ₹ implied rate through forward premium /
discount may be used as both coupon and discount rate.
11.
Bills Rediscounted (DUPN)
Overnight call money rate may be used as both the yield and coupon rates.
12.
Others (specify)

B.


13.
Other Products (Interest Rate
Derivatives)
Derivatives to be converted into positions in the relevant underlying. The amounts considered would
be the principal amount of the underlying or of the notional underlying. Options (where permitted)
children.summary: Sets bucketing treatment for term loans, NPAs, fixed assets, select balance sheet items, and certain money market/forex products. Term loans are sensitive on re-pricing or maturity (earlier) with repricing estimation for BPLR/Base Rate-linked loans; NPAs are sensitive with sub-standard slotted in 1–3 years and doubtful/loss in 3–5 years, coupon taken as zero, and FIMMDA unrated/default yield curve used. It also classifies fixed assets and inter-office adjustments as non-sensitive, leased assets as sensitive on cash flows (bucketed by cash flow dates with FIMMDA corporate bond valuation curve and average coupon approach), reverse repos as sensitive by remaining maturity (FBIL-NSE MIBOR curve), forex swaps as sensitive using implied INR rate for coupon/discounting, and prescribes overnight call money rate for bills rediscounted (DUPN); introduces treatment principle for interest rate derivatives (convert to underlying/notional; options by delta).
children.description: Bucketing and valuation curve guidance for NPAs, fixed/leased assets, repos/forex swaps, and initial principles for interest rate derivatives.

children.title: Annex - VII: Guidance on Bucketing (p.174)
children.text:
shall be considered according to the delta equivalent amount of the underlying or of the notional
underlying.
Actual modified duration for each contract may be computed using the contracted rate as coupon
and the relevant yield curve for discounting factor.
Alternatively all interest rate derivatives can also be dealt with in the following manner:
i)
FRAs
Forward Rate Agreements (FRAs) could also be considered as a combination of a short position
and a long position. For instance, a long position in a September three month FRA (taken on June
1), can be bucketed as a short position in a bond with a maturity of 6 months and a long position in
a bond with a maturity of 3 months. Accordingly a liability in the 3-6 months bucket and an asset in
the 28 days to 3 months bucket may be shown. The amount to be reckoned for computing interest
rate sensitivity is the notional value of the FRA.
ii)
Swaps
Interest Rate Swaps could be considered as a combination of a short position and a long position.
The notional of the fixed and floating leg of an Interest Rate Swap could be shown in the respective
maturity bucket based on the maturity date for the fixed leg and the reset date for the floating leg.
Suppose a bank receives 5-year fixed and pays floating MIBOR, then the fixed leg of the swap could
be shown as an asset in the '5-7 year' bucket and the floating leg would be shown as a liability in
'1-28days' bucket. Similarly, a currency swap may be considered as a combination of a short
position in one currency and long position in another currency. The two positions will be sensitive
to the changes in the respective interest rates. The notionals of the two currencies will be bucketed
as a short / long positions in the respective currency with relevant maturity.
iii)
Futures
Interest Rate Futures (IRFs) could also be considered as a combination of a short position and long
position. For instance, a long position in a September three month IRF (taken on June 1), can be
bucketed as a long position in Government bond, with a maturity of six months and a short position
in Government bond with maturity of three months. The amount to be reckoned for computing
interest rate sensitivity is the notional value of the IRF.
*
Net of provisions, interest suspense and claims received from ECGC / DICGC.
Note:
1. Wherever FIMMDA spreads are proposed to be used, the FIMMDA Corporate Bond Spreads table may be used. The same can be downloaded
from the FIMMDA website (www.fimmda.org).
2. Equity holding whether strategic or for investment purposes may be treated as Non-sensitive and bucketed accordingly.
children.summary: Details bucketing methodology for interest rate derivatives, including options, and provides alternative decomposition approaches for FRAs, swaps, and interest rate futures. It requires derivatives to be represented as positions in the underlying/notional (options by delta equivalent), with actual modified duration computed using contracted coupon and relevant discount curve; alternatively, FRAs/IRFs may be split into offsetting short/long bond positions across specific buckets based on contract dates/maturities, and swaps may be bucketed by fixed-leg maturity and floating-leg reset date (e.g., receive 5-year fixed/pay floating MIBOR: asset in 5–7 year bucket and liability in 1–28 days). Notes include using FIMMDA Corporate Bond Spreads table where spreads are required, and permitting equity holdings (strategic or investment) to be treated as non-sensitive; also clarifies NPA amounts are net of provisions, interest suspense, and ECGC/DICGC claims received.
children.description: Operational bucketing rules for interest rate derivatives (FRAs, swaps, futures, options) and related notes on spreads, equity treatment, and netting for NPAs.
topics: interest rate swaps fixed leg maturity floating reset
npa net of provisions ecgc dicgc

doc_name: Reserve Bank of India (Commercial Banks - Internal Ombudsman) Directions, 2026
doc_description: The **Reserve Bank of India (Commercial Banks – Internal Ombudsman) Directions, 2026** (issued under **Section 35A of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949**) prescribe a strengthened internal grievance redress framework through an **Internal Ombudsman (IO)** mechanism. They apply to **commercial banks** having **10 or more banking outlets in India as on March 31, 2025** (and to banks that meet this threshold later, **six months after** meeting it), with specified exclusions as per the definition of “commercial banks.” The Directions cover the **establishment and functioning of the IO/Dy. IO office**, definitions of complaints/deficiency in service/customer, and processes to ensure **review of complaints before rejection** by an apex-level authority within the bank for faster, meaningful resolution. They are **effective immediately from January 14, 2026**, except **clause 7(2), 14(2) and 14(4)** which must be complied with **by June 30, 2026**, and they also provide RBI a power to **suspend** provisions (and extend such suspension) for specified periods/entities.

structure.title: Reserve Bank of India (Commercial Banks - Internal Ombudsman) Directions, 2026
structure.summary: Provides the title page reference to the "Reserve Bank of India (Commercial Banks - Internal Ombudsman) Directions, 2026". Indicates the instrument is an RBI direction applicable to commercial banks and relates to the Internal Ombudsman framework.
structure.description: Document identifier and subject matter (internal ombudsman directions for commercial banks)

children.title: Preamble / Enabling Provision (Section 35A of Banking Regulation Act, 1949) and Objective
children.text:
भारतीय įरज़वर् बैंक
RESERVE BANK OF INDIA








RBI/CEPD/2025-26/381
CEPD.PRD.No.S1027/13.01.019/2025-26 January 14, 2026

Reserve Bank of India (Commercial Banks - Internal Ombudsman) Directions,
2026

In exercise of the powers conferred by Section 35A of Banking Regulation Act, 1949,
the Reserve Bank of India, being satisfied that it is necessary and expedient in the
public interest so to do, hereby, issues the Directions hereinafter specified.

These Directions are issued with a view to strengthen the Internal Grievance Redress
mechanism within a bank and ensure a speedy and meaningful resolution of customer
complaints by enabling a review before their rejection, by an apex level authority within
the bank.

Chapter I
Preliminary

1. Short Title and Commencement
(1)
These Directions shall be called the Reserve Bank of India (Commercial Banks
- Internal Ombudsman) Directions, 2026.
(2)
These Directions shall come into force with immediate effect except clause 7(2),
14(2) and 14(4) which shall be complied with, latest by June 30, 2026.

2. Suspension
(1)
The Reserve Bank, if it is satisfied that it is expedient so to do, may, by an order,
suspend for such period as may be specified in the order, the operation of any or all
of the provisions of these Directions, either generally or in relation to any specified
regulated entity.
(2)
The Reserve Bank may by an order, extend from time to time, the period of any
suspension ordered as aforesaid by such period, as it may deem fit.
children.summary: Issues the Reserve Bank of India (Commercial Banks - Internal Ombudsman) Directions, 2026 under Section 35A of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949 in public interest. States the objective to strengthen banks’ internal grievance redress mechanism by enabling an apex-level internal review before rejection of customer complaints, to ensure speedy and meaningful resolution. Also sets out commencement (immediate effect with specified deferred clauses) and RBI’s power to suspend provisions.
children.description: Enabling provision, objective, commencement and RBI suspension powers for the Internal Ombudsman Directions

structure.title: Chapter I – Preliminary
structure.summary: Introduces Chapter I (Preliminary) of the Directions, which typically sets out foundational provisions such as title/commencement, suspension, and applicability. Establishes the initial legal-operational context for implementing the Internal Ombudsman framework.
structure.description: Preliminary chapter setting the foundational scope and operation of the directions

children.title: 1. Short Title and Commencement
children.summary: Specifies the short title of the Directions and the date or manner in which they come into force (commencement). Establishes how the Directions should be cited and when compliance obligations begin.
children.description: Citation and effective date/commencement provision

children.title: (1) Short title
children.text:
भारतीय įरज़वर् बैंक
RESERVE BANK OF INDIA








RBI/CEPD/2025-26/381
CEPD.PRD.No.S1027/13.01.019/2025-26 January 14, 2026

Reserve Bank of India (Commercial Banks - Internal Ombudsman) Directions,
2026

In exercise of the powers conferred by Section 35A of Banking Regulation Act, 1949,
the Reserve Bank of India, being satisfied that it is necessary and expedient in the
public interest so to do, hereby, issues the Directions hereinafter specified.

These Directions are issued with a view to strengthen the Internal Grievance Redress
mechanism within a bank and ensure a speedy and meaningful resolution of customer
complaints by enabling a review before their rejection, by an apex level authority within
the bank.

Chapter I
Preliminary

1. Short Title and Commencement
(1)
These Directions shall be called the Reserve Bank of India (Commercial Banks
- Internal Ombudsman) Directions, 2026.
(2)
These Directions shall come into force with immediate effect except clause 7(2),
14(2) and 14(4) which shall be complied with, latest by June 30, 2026.

2. Suspension
(1)
The Reserve Bank, if it is satisfied that it is expedient so to do, may, by an order,
suspend for such period as may be specified in the order, the operation of any or all
of the provisions of these Directions, either generally or in relation to any specified
regulated entity.
(2)
The Reserve Bank may by an order, extend from time to time, the period of any
suspension ordered as aforesaid by such period, as it may deem fit.
children.summary: Provides the short title of the Directions as the Reserve Bank of India (Commercial Banks - Internal Ombudsman) Directions, 2026. It forms part of the preliminary provisions establishing the formal name of the regulatory instrument.
children.description: Short title provision naming the Internal Ombudsman Directions, 2026

children.title: (2) Commencement; deferred compliance for clause 7(2), 14(2) and 14(4) up to June 30, 2026
children.text:
भारतीय įरज़वर् बैंक
RESERVE BANK OF INDIA








RBI/CEPD/2025-26/381
CEPD.PRD.No.S1027/13.01.019/2025-26 January 14, 2026

Reserve Bank of India (Commercial Banks - Internal Ombudsman) Directions,
2026

In exercise of the powers conferred by Section 35A of Banking Regulation Act, 1949,
the Reserve Bank of India, being satisfied that it is necessary and expedient in the
public interest so to do, hereby, issues the Directions hereinafter specified.

These Directions are issued with a view to strengthen the Internal Grievance Redress
mechanism within a bank and ensure a speedy and meaningful resolution of customer
complaints by enabling a review before their rejection, by an apex level authority within
the bank.

Chapter I
Preliminary

1. Short Title and Commencement
(1)
These Directions shall be called the Reserve Bank of India (Commercial Banks
- Internal Ombudsman) Directions, 2026.
(2)
These Directions shall come into force with immediate effect except clause 7(2),
14(2) and 14(4) which shall be complied with, latest by June 30, 2026.

2. Suspension
(1)
The Reserve Bank, if it is satisfied that it is expedient so to do, may, by an order,
suspend for such period as may be specified in the order, the operation of any or all
of the provisions of these Directions, either generally or in relation to any specified
regulated entity.
(2)
The Reserve Bank may by an order, extend from time to time, the period of any
suspension ordered as aforesaid by such period, as it may deem fit.
children.summary: Specifies that the Directions come into force with immediate effect, except clause 7(2), 14(2) and 14(4) which must be complied with latest by June 30, 2026. Establishes the effective date and the deferred compliance timeline for specified clauses.
children.description: Commencement and deferred compliance timeline (up to June 30, 2026) for specified clauses

children.title: 2. Suspension
children.summary: Sets out the suspension provision, indicating whether and how prior instructions/directions on the Internal Ombudsman mechanism are suspended, and the extent of such suspension. Clarifies continuity/transition in regulatory requirements upon issuance of the 2026 Directions.
children.description: Suspension/transition clause for prior internal ombudsman instructions

children.title: (1) RBI power to suspend operation of provisions (generally or for specified RE) for specified period

children.description: RBI authority to suspend operation of the Directions (general or entity-specific) for a specified period

children.title: (2) RBI power to extend suspension from time to time

children.description: RBI power to extend the duration of a suspension order from time to time

children.title: 3. Applicability
children.summary: Defines the applicability of the Directions by specifying the regulated entities covered (commercial banks) and any inclusions/exclusions. Establishes which banks must implement the Internal Ombudsman mechanism under these Directions.
children.description: Scope clause identifying which entities the directions apply to

children.title: (1) Applicable to Commercial Banks with 10 or more banking outlets in India as on March 31, 2025; definition/exclusions for 'Commercial Banks'
children.text:
3. Applicability
(1)
These Directions shall be applicable to Commercial Banks (hereinafter
collectively referred to as 'banks' and individually as a 'bank') having 10 or more
banking outlets in India as on March 31, 2025, whether such bank is incorporated in
India or outside India.
For the purpose of these Directions, ‘Commercial Banks’ mean banking companies
(other than Small Finance Banks, Payment Banks, and Local Area Banks),
corresponding new banks, and the State Bank of India, as defined respectively under
clauses (c), (da), and (nc) of Section 5 of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949, but does
not include a bank in resolution or winding up or under directions.
(2)
The directions shall also be applicable to the commercial bank, which meets
the above mentioned criteria after March 31, 2025, with effect from six months from
meeting the eligibility criteria.

4. Definitions
(1)
In these Directions, unless the context states otherwise, the terms herein shall
bear the meanings assigned to them as below:
(a)
“Banking Outlet” is a fixed-point service delivery unit as defined under
the Reserve Bank of India (Commercial Banks - Branch Authorisation)
Directions, 2025 issued by Reserve Bank of India, and as amended from time
to time;
(b)
“Competent Authority” means Whole Time Director / Executive Directorin-charge of customer service vertical of the bank;
(c)
“Complaint” means a representation in writing or through other modes
alleging deficiency in service on the part of the bank with or without seeking
relief thereon;
(d)
“Credit Information Company (CIC)” means a company as defined in the
Companies Act, 2013 and has been granted a certificate of registration under
sub-section (2) of section 5 of the Credit Information Companies (Regulation)
Act, 2005;
(e)
“Customer” means a person who uses, or is an applicant for, a service
provided by the bank;
children.summary: Sets applicability to Commercial Banks having 10 or more banking outlets in India as on March 31, 2025, whether incorporated in India or outside India. Defines 'Commercial Banks' as banking companies other than SFBs, Payment Banks and Local Area Banks, and includes corresponding new banks and SBI as per Section 5 of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949; excludes banks in resolution/winding up/under directions.
children.description: Applicability threshold (≥10 banking outlets as on March 31, 2025) and definition/exclusions for ‘commercial banks’

children.title: (2) Applicability to banks meeting criteria after March 31, 2025 (effective after six months of meeting criteria)

children.description: Onboarding rule for banks crossing the eligibility threshold after March 31, 2025 (effective after 6 months)

children.title: 4. Definitions
children.summary: Sets out the definitions used in the Directions for consistent interpretation across chapters, including key institutional terms and roles relevant to the Internal Ombudsman framework. Establishes the meaning of defined expressions that govern applicability, governance, and complaint-handling requirements.
children.description: Glossary of defined terms used throughout the Internal Ombudsman Directions

children.title: (1) Definitions (a) to (j)
children.summary: Provides specific definitions for items (a) to (j), clarifying the meaning of core terms used in the Directions (e.g., entities covered, complaint-related terminology, and roles/responsibilities). These definitions standardise how banks must interpret and apply procedural and governance requirements under the Internal Ombudsman mechanism.

children.summary: Defines 'Customer' as a person who uses, or is an applicant for, a service provided by the bank. This sets the eligible complainant base for the grievance redress and internal ombudsman process.
children.description: Definition of ‘customer’ (service user or applicant) for complaint handling coverage

children.title: (f) Deficiency in service
children.text:
(f)
“Deficiency in service” means a shortcoming or an inadequacy in any
service, which the bank is required to provide statutorily or otherwise, which
may or may not result in financial loss or damage to the customer;
(g)
“Deputy Internal Ombudsman (Dy. IO)” means any person appointed
under clause 6 of these Directions;
(h)
“Financial Sector Regulatory Body” means regulatory body for financial
sector entities and includes:
(i) The Reserve Bank of India established under the Reserve Bank of
India Act, 1934;
(ii) The Securities and Exchange Board of India established under the
Securities and Exchange Board of India Act, 1992;
(iii) The Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India
established under the Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority
of India Act, 1999;
(iv) The Pension Fund Regulatory and Development Authority
established under the Pension Fund Regulatory and Development
Authority Act, 2013;
(i)
“Internal Ombudsman (IO)” means any person appointed under clause
5 of these Directions;
(j)
“Regulated Entity (RE)” means a commercial bank or payments bank or
small finance bank or a non-banking financial company or a non-bank prepaid
payment instrument issuer or a credit information company which are covered
under the purview of the Internal Ombudsman framework, or any other entity
as may be specified by the Reserve Bank from time to time.

(2) All other expressions, unless defined herein, shall have the same meaning as
assigned to them under the Banking Regulation Act, 1949, the Reserve Bank of
India Act, 1934, the Payment and Settlement Systems Act, 2007, the Credit
Information Companies (Regulation) Act, 2005, the Credit Information Companies
Rules, 2006, the Credit Information Companies Regulations, 2006, or the Reserve
Bank - Integrated Ombudsman Scheme (as amended from time to time) or
regulations, directions and guidelines issued by the Reserve Bank of India.
children.summary: Defines key terms including 'Deficiency in service' (shortcoming/inadequacy in any service the bank is required to provide, with or without financial loss), 'Deputy Internal Ombudsman (Dy. IO)', 'Financial Sector Regulatory Body' (RBI, SEBI, IRDAI, PFRDA), 'Internal Ombudsman (IO)', and 'Regulated Entity (RE)' (entities covered under the Internal Ombudsman framework, including commercial banks, payment banks, SFBs, NBFCs, non-bank PPI issuers, and CICs). Also provides that undefined expressions take meanings from specified statutes and the RBI Integrated Ombudsman Scheme and related RBI regulations/directions.
children.description: Core definitions for service deficiency, IO/Dy.IO roles, covered regulated entities, and interpretive cross-references

structure.title: Chapter II – Office of the Internal Ombudsman
structure.summary: Introduces the framework for the Office of the Internal Ombudsman, covering its establishment within banks, its role in strengthening the internal grievance redressal process, and the broad operational and governance expectations. Lays down the overarching structure within which the Internal Ombudsman function is to operate, including independence and process integration with complaint handling.
structure.description: Chapter establishing the Internal Ombudsman office framework and its operating principles

children.title: 5. Appointment of Internal Ombudsman
children.summary: Prescribes requirements for appointment of the Internal Ombudsman, including the process and governance around selection to ensure independence and effective oversight of complaint handling. Sets expectations on how banks must appoint and position the Internal Ombudsman within the organisation to support impartial review of grievance redressal decisions.
children.description: Appointment process and governance requirements for selecting an Internal Ombudsman

children.title: (1) Eligibility/experience; serving officer to relinquish before assuming charge (Proviso)
children.text:
Chapter II
Office of the Internal Ombudsman

5. Appointment of Internal Ombudsman
(1)
The IO shall either be a retired or serving officer, in the rank equivalent to a
General Manager in the RE of the Reserve Bank under the purview of the Internal
Ombudsman framework or a Financial Sector Regulatory Body, having necessary
skills and experience of minimum seven years of working in areas such as banking,
non-banking finance, regulation, supervision, payment and settlement systems, credit
information or consumer protection.
Provided that, if the person is a serving officer, he / she is required to relinquish
the same before assuming charge as IO.
(2)
The IO shall previously not have been employed, nor presently be employed,
by the bank or a holding, associate or subsidiary company of the bank.
(3)
The IO shall not be over 70 years of age before the completion of the tenure.
(4)
A person may work as the IO in more than one RE simultaneously at the
discretion of the REs concerned, subject to the approval of the Board or Customer
Service Committee / Consumer Protection Committee of the Board of the appointing
RE.

6. Appointment of Deputy Internal Ombudsman
(1)
The Dy. IO shall either be a retired or serving officer, in the rank equivalent to
a Deputy General Manager in the RE under the purview of the Internal Ombudsman
framework or a Financial Sector Regulatory Body, having necessary skills and
experience of minimum five years of working in areas such as banking, non-banking
finance, regulation, supervision, payment and settlement systems, credit information
or consumer protection.
Provided that, if the person is a serving officer, he / she is required to relinquish
the same before assuming charge as Dy. IO.
(2)
The Dy. IO shall previously not have been employed, nor presently be
employed, by the bank or a holding, associate or subsidiary company of the bank.
(3)
The Dy. IO shall not be over 70 years of age before the completion of the tenure.
(4)
The Dy. IO shall not be employed in more than one RE simultaneously.
children.summary: Sets eligibility criteria for appointment of the Internal Ombudsman (IO) and Deputy Internal Ombudsman (Dy. IO), including rank equivalence (IO: General Manager; Dy. IO: Deputy General Manager) and minimum relevant experience (IO: 7 years; Dy. IO: 5 years) in specified domains (banking/NBFC/regulation/supervision/payments/credit information/consumer protection). It requires serving officers to relinquish their existing position before assuming charge, prescribes an age cap (not over 70 before completion of tenure), and permits an IO (not Dy. IO) to serve more than one RE simultaneously subject to Board/Board committee approval.
children.description: Appointment eligibility, experience, and key conditions for IO and Dy. IO (rank, experience, relinquishment, age, multi-RE engagement)

children.title: (2) Independence: not previously/presently employed by the bank or its holding/associate/subsidiary

children.description: Independence requirement barring IO/Dy. IO from past or present employment with the bank or its group entities

children.description: Provision permitting an IO to serve multiple REs with Board/Board committee approval

children.title: 6. Appointment of Deputy Internal Ombudsman
children.summary: Provides for appointment of a Deputy Internal Ombudsman (DIO) by the Regulated Entity to support the Internal Ombudsman function. Sets out the requirement to have an appropriate appointment framework and role alignment so that the DIO can assist in handling customer complaints under the Internal Ombudsman mechanism.
children.description: Framework for appointing a deputy internal ombudsman to support the internal ombudsman function

The Dy. IO shall not be over 70 years of age before the completion of the tenure.
(4)
The Dy. IO shall not be employed in more than one RE simultaneously.
children.summary: Reiterates the independence requirement for IO and Dy. IO appointments: the appointee must not have been employed earlier or be currently employed by the bank or its holding, associate, or subsidiary company. This is intended to avoid conflicts of interest in complaint review under the Internal Ombudsman mechanism.
children.description: Independence and conflict-of-interest bar for IO/Dy. IO vis-à-vis the bank and its group companies

tables.raw_text:
Office of the Internal Ombudsman | 
 | 
5. Appointment of Internal Ombudsman | 
(1) The IO shall either be a retired or serving officer, in the rank equivalent to a | 
General Manager in the RE of the Reserve Bank under the purview of the Internal | 
Ombudsman framework or a Financial Sector Regulatory Body, having necessary | 
skills and experience of minimum seven years of working in areas such as banking, | 
non-banking finance, regulation, supervision, payment and settlement systems, credit | 
information or consumer protection. | 
 | Provided that, if the person is a serving officer, he / she is required to relinquish
 | the same before assuming charge as IO.
(2) The IO shall previously not have been employed, nor presently be employed, | 
by the bank or a holding, associate or subsidiary company of the bank. | 
(3) The IO shall not be over 70 years of age before the completion of the tenure. | 
(4) A person may work as the IO in more than one RE simultaneously at the | 
discretion of the REs concerned, subject to the approval of the Board or Customer | 
Service Committee / Consumer Protection Committee of the Board of the appointing | 
RE. | 
 | 
6. Appointment of Deputy Internal Ombudsman | 
(1) The Dy. IO shall either be a retired or serving officer, in the rank equivalent to | 
a Deputy General Manager in the RE under the purview of the Internal Ombudsman | 
framework or a Financial Sector Regulatory Body, having necessary skills and | 
experience of minimum five years of working in areas such as banking, non-banking | 
finance, regulation, supervision, payment and settlement systems, credit information | 
or consumer protection. | 
 | Provided that, if the person is a serving officer, he / she is required to relinquish
 | the same before assuming charge as Dy. IO.
(2) The Dy. IO shall previously not have been employed, nor presently be | 
employed, by the bank or a holding, associate or subsidiary company of the bank. | 
(3) The Dy. IO shall not be over 70 years of age before the completion of the tenure. | 
(4) The Dy. IO shall not be employed in more than one RE simultaneously. | 
 |

children.title: (3) Age limit: not over 70 before completion of tenure
children.text:
7. Number of Internal Ombudsman / Deputy Internal Ombudsman
(1)
Every bank shall appoint at least one IO.
(2)
The Customer Service Committee of the Board of the bank shall determine, at
least once in a year, the number of IO/ Dy. IO to be appointed having due regard to
volume and complexity of the complaints received, and ensuring that the IO/ Dy. IO
get sufficient time to apply his/her mind on the principles of fairness, equity and natural
justice while reviewing the resolution provided by the bank.
(3)
While appointing additional IO / Dy. IO, the bank shall consider the need for
diversity of experience of the incumbents to deal with different types of cases. In such
cases, the bank may clearly define the jurisdiction of each IO / Dy. IO.

8. Tenure of Internal Ombudsman / Deputy Internal Ombudsman
(1) The appointment of the IO / Dy. IO in the bank shall be contractual.
(2)
The bank shall ensure that the post of the IO does not remain vacant at any
point of time. During the temporary absence of the IO, the Dy. IO may function as the
IO.
(3)
In a rare case where both IO/s and Dy IO/s are on leave / absent, the bank may
designate its serving official equivalent to the General Manager rank as the IO for a
period not exceeding one month, with the approval of the Customer Service
Committee of the Board. Such official shall not have any reporting relationship with the
business verticals of the bank during the period in which he/she is designated as the
IO.
(4)
The tenure of the IO / Dy. IO in the bank shall be a fixed term of not less than
three years. However, the total tenure (including extension/reappointment, if any) of
the IO / Dy. IO in the bank shall not exceed five years.
(5)
To fill a vacancy, the bank shall undertake the process of fresh appointment at
least three months in advance of the expiry of the tenure of the incumbent IO and
ensure that there is a reasonable overlap between the time of demitting of office of the
outgoing IO and the incoming IO.
(6) The IO / Dy. IO shall not be removed before the completion of his/her
contracted term without the approval of the Customer Service Committee of the Board.
In case a vacancy arises on account of reasons beyond the control of the bank (such
children.summary: Prescribes governance for the number and tenure of IO/Dy. IO: every bank must appoint at least one IO, and the Customer Service Committee of the Board must review at least annually the required number based on complaint volume/complexity and fairness/equity/natural justice considerations. It sets contractual appointments, mandates that the IO post must not remain vacant (Dy. IO may act during temporary absence), permits a temporary designation of a serving GM-rank official as IO for up to one month with committee approval (with no reporting relationship to business verticals), fixes tenure at not less than 3 years and total tenure not exceeding 5 years, requires initiating fresh appointment at least 3 months before expiry with reasonable overlap, and restricts removal before term completion without committee approval.
children.description: Requirements on minimum IO presence, annual staffing review, vacancy management, and fixed-term tenure/renewal/removal controls
topics: temporary designation up to one month

children.title: (4) Dy. IO not to be employed in more than one RE simultaneously

children.description: Restriction prohibiting Dy. IO from simultaneous employment across multiple regulated entities

get sufficient time to apply his/her mind on the principles of fairness, equity and natural
justice while reviewing the resolution provided by the bank.
(3)
While appointing additional IO / Dy. IO, the bank shall consider the need for
diversity of experience of the incumbents to deal with different types of cases. In such
cases, the bank may clearly define the jurisdiction of each IO / Dy. IO.

8. Tenure of Internal Ombudsman / Deputy Internal Ombudsman
(1) The appointment of the IO / Dy. IO in the bank shall be contractual.
(2)
The bank shall ensure that the post of the IO does not remain vacant at any
point of time. During the temporary absence of the IO, the Dy. IO may function as the
IO.
(3)
In a rare case where both IO/s and Dy IO/s are on leave / absent, the bank may
designate its serving official equivalent to the General Manager rank as the IO for a
period not exceeding one month, with the approval of the Customer Service
Committee of the Board. Such official shall not have any reporting relationship with the
business verticals of the bank during the period in which he/she is designated as the
IO.
(4)
The tenure of the IO / Dy. IO in the bank shall be a fixed term of not less than
three years. However, the total tenure (including extension/reappointment, if any) of
the IO / Dy. IO in the bank shall not exceed five years.
(5)
To fill a vacancy, the bank shall undertake the process of fresh appointment at
least three months in advance of the expiry of the tenure of the incumbent IO and
ensure that there is a reasonable overlap between the time of demitting of office of the
outgoing IO and the incoming IO.
(6) The IO / Dy. IO shall not be removed before the completion of his/her
contracted term without the approval of the Customer Service Committee of the Board.
In case a vacancy arises on account of reasons beyond the control of the bank (such
children.summary: To fill a vacancy, the bank must initiate the process of fresh appointment at least three months before the incumbent IO’s tenure expiry. The bank must also ensure a reasonable overlap between the outgoing IO and incoming IO to support continuity.
children.description: Advance succession planning for IO vacancies, including a 3-month lead time and overlap requirement.

children.title: (6) No removal before completion without Committee approval; RBI intimation within 10 working days for vacancy; appoint new IO/Dy. IO within three months

(1)
Every bank shall appoint at least one IO.
(2)
The Customer Service Committee of the Board of the bank shall determine, at
least once in a year, the number of IO/ Dy. IO to be appointed having due regard to
volume and complexity of the complaints received, and ensuring that the IO/ Dy. IO
get sufficient time to apply his/her mind on the principles of fairness, equity and natural

children.summary: Provides that the IO/Dy. IO shall not be removed before completion of the contracted term without approval of the Customer Service Committee of the Board. Where a vacancy arises due to reasons beyond the bank’s control (e.g., resignation, incapacitation, illness, death), the bank must inform RBI within 10 working days and appoint a new IO/Dy. IO (as per eligibility criteria) within three months from the date of vacancy.
children.description: Controls premature removal and sets RBI notification (10 working days) and replacement appointment (3 months) timelines for IO/Dy. IO vacancies.
topics: vacancy due to resignation illness death

children.title: (7) Emoluments/benefits set by Committee; not to be changed adversely during tenure
children.text:
as resignation, incapacitation, illness, death, etc.), the bank shall inform Reserve Bank
of India within 10 working days from the date of such vacancy and shall appoint a new
IO / Dy. IO as per eligibility criteria specified under clause 5 and clause 6 of these
Directions within three months from the date of vacancy.
(7) The Customer Service Committee of the Board of the bank shall determine the
structure of emoluments, facilities and benefits accorded to the IO / Dy. IO, which
should be appropriate keeping in view the stature and position of the IO / Dy. IO being
at the apex of the grievance redress mechanism of the bank as also the need to attract
experienced persons with requisite expertise. These emoluments, facilities and
benefits accorded to the IO / Dy. IO, once determined, shall not be changed adversely
during the tenure of the IO / Dy. IO.

9. Administrative Oversight
(1)
The IO shall report to the Competent Authority, as defined under clause 4(1)(b)
of the Directions, of the bank administratively, and to the Customer Service Committee
of the Board of the bank functionally.

10. Secretariat of the office of Internal Ombudsman
(1)
The bank shall provide such number of its officers and staff to the office of the
IO as is considered necessary for the smooth functioning of the office of the IO.
(2)
All other requisite office infrastructure, including information technology support
shall be made available to the office of the IO to enable the IO/ Dy. IO to discharge
the responsibilities effectively and efficiently.
(3)
The office of the IO may preferably be placed in the Head Office or Corporate
Office of the bank.

11. Internal Audit
(1)
The Internal Audit Department of the bank shall conduct an audit of the
implementation of these Directions on an annual basis, covering, inter-alia:
(a) The process of appointment / reappointment of the IO/Dy. IO, adequacy of
the human resources and infrastructure provided to the office of the IO in
relation to the volume of complaints;
children.summary: Requires the Customer Service Committee of the Board to determine the emoluments, facilities and benefits for the IO/Dy. IO, keeping in view the apex role in the bank’s grievance redress mechanism and the need to attract experienced persons. Once determined, these emoluments, facilities and benefits shall not be changed adversely during the tenure of the IO/Dy. IO.
children.description: Board Committee sets IO/Dy. IO compensation and protects it from adverse change during tenure.
topics: customer service committee of the board

children.summary: Covers the requirement for an internal audit framework to assess compliance with the Internal Ombudsman (IO) mechanism and related RBI directions. It sets expectations that the IO framework and its functioning are subject to periodic audit and reporting to ensure adherence to prescribed processes and governance.
children.description: Internal audit requirements to test compliance and effectiveness of the internal ombudsman mechanism

children.title: (1) Annual audit of implementation covering (a) to (d)
children.summary: Prescribes an annual audit of implementation of the IO framework, covering specified areas (a) to (d) such as adherence to the IO process, quality and timeliness of complaint handling, and compliance with the directions. The section focuses on ensuring the annual audit explicitly verifies implementation against the mandated components and documents gaps for corrective action.
children.description: Annual audit scope for verifying implementation of the internal ombudsman framework across specified parameters

IO and the adequacy of human resources and infrastructure provided to the IO office relative to complaint volumes. This sets internal assurance expectations around governance and resourcing of the IO framework.
children.description: Annual internal audit coverage for IO/Dy. IO appointment processes and adequacy of staffing/infrastructure.

children.title: (b) Auto-escalation implementation within timelines
children.text:
(b) Implementation of auto-escalation of the partially resolved or wholly rejected
complaints to the office of the IO within the timelines;
(c) Action taken by the office of the IO with regard to analysis of complaints,
reports submitted to the Reserve Bank of India and the bank, raising awareness
of the staff of the bank about the grievance redressal processes, and such other
processes;
(d) Submission of the information related to appointment of the IO / Dy. IO and
submission of periodic report on the functioning of the IO by the bank to the
Reserve Bank.
(2)
The scope of the internal audit shall exclude any assessment of the correctness
of decisions taken by the IO / Dy. IO.

IO appointment information and periodic functioning reports. It also clarifies that internal audit scope shall exclude assessment of the correctness of decisions taken by the IO/Dy. IO.
children.description: Defines internal audit checks for auto-escalation, IO office actions/reporting (including RBI submissions), while excluding review of IO decision correctness.

It also includes checking bank submissions to RBI regarding IO/Dy. IO appointments and periodic functioning reports. structure.title: Chapter III – Role and Responsibilities
structure.summary: Introduces the chapter detailing the roles and responsibilities of key stakeholders in the IO framework, including the Internal Ombudsman/Deputy Internal Ombudsman and the Board. It outlines accountability and governance expectations for effective functioning of the internal grievance redress mechanism.
structure.description: Chapter setting out stakeholder responsibilities and accountability for the internal ombudsman framework

children.title: 12. Role and Responsibilities of Internal Ombudsman / Deputy Internal Ombudsman
children.summary: Specifies the role and responsibilities of the Internal Ombudsman/Deputy Internal Ombudsman in reviewing customer complaints and ensuring fair, consistent, and compliant resolution before final closure by the regulated entity. It focuses on independence in decision-making within the internal grievance redress process and adherence to the prescribed operating procedure.
children.description: Duties and accountability of the internal ombudsman/deputy internal ombudsman in complaint review and resolution

children.title: (1) IO office not to handle complaints received directly; handles partially resolved/wholly rejected complaints already examined by bank
children.text:
Chapter – III
Role and Responsibilities

12. Role and Responsibilities of Internal Ombudsman / Deputy Internal
Ombudsman
(1)
The office of the IO shall not handle complaints received directly from the
complainants or members of the public. It shall deal with the complaints that have
already been examined by the bank but have been partially resolved or being wholly
rejected by the bank.
(2)
The IO / Dy. IO shall not represent the bank in legal cases before any court or
fora or authority.
(3)
While the IO may decide any or all complaints, the power for the Dy. IO to
decide the complaints may be defined under a policy approved by the Customer
Service Committee of the Board.
(4)
The IO / Dy. IO shall recommend suitable compensation to the complainant, as
per the compensation prescribed by the Reserve Bank of India in its extant guidelines,
if any, and as per the compensation policy of the bank, if any, in case there is no
prescription from the Reserve Bank of India.
(5)
The IO may recommend compensation in accordance with the Reserve BankIntegrated Ombudsman Scheme, as amended from time to time, for any consequential
loss and the loss of time, expenses incurred and harassment / mental agony suffered
by the complainant, over and above the compensation recommended in clause 12(4).
(6)
The office of IO shall, on a quarterly basis, analyse the pattern of all complaints
received against the bank, such as product-wise, category-wise, consumer groupwise, geographical location-wise, etc., and provide inputs to the bank for policy
intervention, if so warranted.
(7)
The IO shall suggest means for taking actions to address the root cause of
complaints of similar / repeat nature and those that require policy level changes in the
bank.
(8)
The IO/ Dy. IO shall have ‘read-only’ access to the Reserve Bank’s Complaint
Management System to enable them to keep abreast of decisions of the RBI
Ombudsman / Appellate Authority. The bank shall seek such access for the IO / Dy.
IO from the Consumer Education and Protection Department of the Reserve Bank.
(9)
The Dy. IO shall functionally report to the IO.
children.summary: Provides that the IO office must not handle complaints received directly from complainants or the public; it may deal only with complaints already examined by the bank that are partially resolved or proposed to be wholly rejected. It also sets out key IO/Dy. IO responsibilities including compensation recommendations, quarterly complaint pattern analysis for policy inputs, root-cause action suggestions, read-only access to RBI’s Complaint Management System (CMS), and functional reporting of Dy. IO to IO.
children.description: Core role definition for the IO office—eligible complaint universe and key responsibilities in grievance redressal oversight.

children.title: (2) IO/Dy. IO not to represent bank in legal cases

Ombudsman / Appellate Authority. The bank shall seek such access for the IO / Dy.
IO from the Consumer Education and Protection Department of the Reserve Bank.
(9)
The Dy. IO shall functionally report to the IO.
children.summary: Prohibits the Internal Ombudsman/Deputy Internal Ombudsman from representing the bank in legal cases before any court, forum, or authority. This restriction is part of the defined role and responsibilities framework for the IO function.
children.description: Bar on IO/Dy. IO acting as the bank’s legal representative in external proceedings.

Chapter – III
Role and Responsibilities

12. Role and Responsibilities of Internal Ombudsman / Deputy Internal
Ombudsman
(1)
The office of the IO shall not handle complaints received directly from the
complainants or members of the public. It shall deal with the complaints that have
already been examined by the bank but have been partially resolved or being wholly
rejected by the bank.
(2)

fora or authority.
(3)

decide the complaints may be defined under a policy approved by the Customer

per the compensation prescribed by the Reserve Bank of India in its extant guidelines,
if any, and as per the compensation policy of the bank, if any, in case there is no
prescription from the Reserve Bank of India.
(5)
The IO may recommend compensation in accordance with the Reserve BankIntegrated Ombudsman Scheme, as amended from time to time, for any consequential
loss and the loss of time, expenses incurred and harassment / mental agony suffered
by the complainant, over and above the compensation recommended in clause 12(4).

children.description: Functional reporting relationship of Dy. IO to IO.

children.title: 13. Board Oversight
children.summary: Sets out Board oversight responsibilities for the IO mechanism, including ensuring an effective governance structure, monitoring performance and compliance, and reviewing periodic reports on the functioning of the IO framework. It emphasizes Board-level accountability for implementation, corrective actions, and sustained effectiveness of internal grievance redress.
children.description: Board-level governance and monitoring requirements for the internal ombudsman mechanism

children.title: (1) IO permanent invitee to Customer Service Committee of the Board; rotation/representation where multiple IOs
children.text:
13. Board Oversight
(1)
The IO shall be designated as a permanent invitee to the meetings of the
Customer Service Committee of the Board. In banks having multiple IOs, a view shall
be taken by the Customer Service Committee of the Board to have representation of
more than one IO or having a system of rotation.
(2)
The IO shall furnish periodic reports (including the analysis of complaints) on
his / her activities to the Customer Service Committee of the Board, preferably at
quarterly, but not less than half yearly, intervals.
(3)
The decision of the IO / Dy. IO can be overruled only with the approval of the
Competent Authority as defined under clause 4(1)(b) of these Directions.
(4)
All such cases where the decision of the IO / Dy. IO has been overruled by the
Competent Authority shall be placed before the Customer Service Committee of the
Board of the bank for review.
(5)
Information on the complaints resolved by the RBI Ombudsman in favour of
complainant, either partially or fully, shall also be placed before the Customer Service
Committee of the Board, on quarterly basis. The information shall be accompanied
with an analysis of minimum top five categories of complaints along with remedial
measures so as to avoid complaints of a similar nature in future.
children.summary: Sets board oversight requirements: the IO must be a permanent invitee to the Customer Service Committee of the Board, with representation/rotation arrangements where multiple IOs exist. It also requires periodic IO reporting to the Committee, restricts overruling of IO/Dy. IO decisions to approval by the Competent Authority, mandates Committee review of overruled cases, and requires quarterly placement of RBI Ombudsman outcomes in favour of complainants along with analysis of at least the top five complaint categories and remedial measures.
children.description: Board-level governance and oversight of the IO function, including committee participation, overruling controls, and quarterly reporting of RBI Ombudsman outcomes.
topics: customer service committee of the board

children.title: (2) Periodic IO reports (incl. analysis) to Committee (preferably quarterly; at least half-yearly)
